title: Connecting Supabase
intro: Your database schema is ready and your queries are written. Now it's time to connect your Next.js task manager to Supabase so your app can actually talk to the database. Think of it like plugging a cable between two devices — one wrong setting and there's no connection.

teach.title: Supabase: Your Backend in a Box
teach.body: Supabase gives you a PostgreSQL database, authentication, and an API — all without building a backend from scratch. Your Next.js app communicates with Supabase using a **client library** that handles the connection, authentication, and query execution. You initialize the client with two keys: a **project URL** and an **anon key** (a public API key that works with Row Level Security).

teach.title: Initializing the Supabase Client
teach.annotation: The keys come from environment variables, not hardcoded strings. NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix makes them available in the browser. The anon key is safe to expose because RLS policies protect your data.

teach.title: Connecting Supabase Is Like Plugging In a USB Cable
teach.body: Your app and your database are two devices that need to talk. The Supabase client library is the cable, the project URL is the port you plug into, and the anon key is like the handshake that says 'yes, this device is allowed to connect.' One wrong setting and there's no connection — but get it right and data flows instantly.

Connecting your app to a real database is the moment your project stops being a prototype and starts being a product. This is the step that makes your task manager capable of serving real users — and real users are what generate real income.

Store Supabase credentials in environment variables, never in your code. The anon key is designed to be public — it's Row Level Security (not the key itself) that protects your data. Always enable RLS on every table.

start.narrative: You've created a Supabase project and have your URL and keys. You need to connect your Next.js task manager to the database. Where do you store the credentials?

choices.text: Paste them directly into your code: const url = 'https://abc123.supabase.co'
Put them in a .env.local file and reference them with process.env
Create a config.json file with the keys and import it

hardcoded.narrative: You push your code to GitHub. Your Supabase URL and keys are now visible in your public repository. A bot scrapes GitHub for exposed keys and starts spamming your database with junk data within minutes. What now?

choices.text: Immediately rotate the keys in Supabase, move them to .env.local, and add .env.local to .gitignore
Delete the commit from GitHub and hope nobody saw it

env-good.narrative: Your .env.local is set up correctly with NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_URL and NEXT_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY. Time to wire up the client.

choices.text: Move on to initializing the Supabase client

config-json.narrative: The config.json gets committed to your repository. Unlike .env.local (which is in .gitignore by default), JSON files are tracked by Git. Your keys are now in version control.

choices.text: Delete config.json, move keys to .env.local, and purge the file from Git history
Just add config.json to .gitignore going forward

init-client.narrative: Credentials are sorted. Now you need to create the Supabase client your components will import. Where does this live?

choices.text: A single lib/supabase.ts file that exports one createClient(...) instance, imported anywhere it's needed
Call createClient(url, key) at the top of every component that needs it
Attach the client to window.supabase so any code can grab it without importing

fetch-pattern.narrative: Client is initialized. Now you need to actually fetch tasks. Where do you make the database call?

choices.text: Directly in your React component using the anon key — RLS policies on the database scope each query to the logged-in user
From an API route using the service role key, manually filtering by user_id in your code
From an API route using the service role key, returning whatever the query gives back

errors.narrative: Queries are flowing. But sometimes the network drops or Supabase returns an error. Right now, when something fails, the UI just shows an empty list. How do you handle errors properly?

choices.text: Destructure { data, error } from every Supabase call. If error is set, log it, show a toast, and keep the previous data visible.
Wrap the call in try/catch — Supabase will throw on failure, right?
Ignore errors — they're rare and an empty list is good enough

rls-policies.narrative: Errors are handled. Time for the security layer. The anon key is public, so anyone with browser dev tools can call your tables directly. What protects your data?

choices.text: Enable Row Level Security on every table and write policies like: USING (auth.uid() = user_id). The database refuses any query that isn't scoped to the requesting user.
Enable RLS but write a permissive policy: USING (true). Counts as 'enabled' on the dashboard.
Skip RLS entirely — your queries already include user_id, that's enough

excellent-end.narrative: Your Supabase connection is production-grade: secrets in env vars, a single client module, anon key with RLS for user-scoped queries, proper error handling, and strict policies on every table. A bug in the frontend can't leak data — the database refuses.
excellent-end.feedback: This is the recommended Supabase pattern: env-based secrets, one shared client, anon key + RLS as your security layer, and disciplined error handling at every call site. The result is an app that's both secure by default and pleasant to debug.

mediocre-end.narrative: Permissive RLS technically passes the dashboard's 'RLS enabled' check, but USING (true) means every authenticated user can read every row. You shipped a security hole disguised as a security feature.
mediocre-end.feedback: RLS without a real policy is worse than no RLS — it gives a false sense of safety. Your policies should explicitly reference auth.uid() (or another identity) so the database actually scopes data to the right user.

weak-end.narrative: Your app works in testing because all your queries include user_id. Then you ship a new feature, forget the filter, and the anon key happily returns everyone's tasks to the browser. There's no database-side guard to catch you.
weak-end.feedback: When your database is reachable from the browser with a public key, RLS isn't optional — it's the whole security model. Application-side filtering is one bug away from a leak. Always pair the anon key with strict policies.
